    Home › Aircraft › Boeing 777

    Boeing 777

    Boeing · Widebody · In production (777X in certification)

    The world's largest twinjet — the long-haul workhorse.

    Length73.86 m
    Wingspan64.8 m
    Range13,649 km
    MTOW352 t
    Typical seats396
    Built1,750+
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#5 of 35
    5.36 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#50 of 65
    38.8 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#36 of 68
    0.88 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#12 of 68
    4.76 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#7 of 65
    14.4 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Weight per seat#10 of 35
    888 kg MTOW ÷ typical seats
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Boeing 777's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Boeing 77764.8 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #6 of 68 by length → #8 by weight → #28 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length (-300ER)73.86 m
    Wingspan64.80 m
    Height18.50 m
    MTOW351,533 kg
    Range13,649 km
    Typical seats396 (2-class)
    Engines2 × GE90-115B
    First flight (777)12 June 1994
    First flight (-300ER)24 February 2003

    Compare it

    Put the Boeing 777 next to something

    vs Airbus A350 →vs Airbus A380 →
    vs Airbus A380 vs Airbus A350 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousBoeing 767 Next →Boeing 777X

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    The 777 is the aircraft that quietly retired the jumbo jet. Boeing designed it in the early 1990s as the first airliner developed entirely on computers, and gave it two engines so large — the GE90's fan is wider than a 737's fuselage — that a twin could finally do what only three- and four-engine aircraft had done before.

    The -300ER variant became the best-selling widebody of its era because the economics were brutal in its favour: near-747 capacity on two engines instead of four. Around 1,750 777s have been built across all variants, and the freighter version carries a huge share of the world's long-haul air cargo.

    The 777X, with folding wingtips and the even larger GE9X, is the family's next chapter — in certification now.

    Common questions

    Boeing 777 — the short answers

    How fast is the Boeing 777?
    The Boeing 777 cruises at about 950 km/h — roughly 513 knots, or 590 mph.
    How far can the Boeing 777 fly?
    The Boeing 777 has a maximum range of about 13,649 km (7,370 nautical miles, 8,483 miles). At its cruise speed that is roughly 14.4 hours in the air.
    How many people does the Boeing 777 carry?
    A typical Boeing 777 carries 396 passengers. Exact figures vary with the operator's chosen configuration.
    Is the Boeing 777 still in production?
    Status: In production (777X in certification). The Boeing 777 first flew in 1994, and about 1,750 have been built.
    How big is the Boeing 777?
    The Boeing 777 is 73.86 m long with a wingspan of 64.8 m and a height of 18.5 m. Its maximum takeoff weight is 351,533 kg.
    Variants

    The Boeing 777 line

    777-200 / -200ER / -200LR
    The originals — the -200LR set the world distance record for a commercial aircraft.
    777-300ER
    The best-selling widebody variant of its era. Spec shown above.
    777F
    The freighter — backbone of long-haul cargo fleets worldwide.
    777-8 / 777-9
    The 777X generation — folding wingtips, GE9X engines, in certification.
    Operators

    Who flies the Boeing 777

    The top 25 operators by fleet size, of ≈65 worldwide — the rest are aggregated below. Counts marked ≈ are approximate, verified 2026-07-14.

    EKEmiratesThe largest 777 fleet in the world — 117 × 777-300ER and 10 × 777-200LR.127✓→ UAUnited AirlinesThe 777's launch operator, 1995 — still flying the original -200s domestically.96→ QRQatar AirwaysPassenger -300ERs plus one of the world's largest 777F freighter fleets; 777-9 launch group.≈90→ AAAmerican Airlines67→ AFAir FranceLaunch customer of the 777-300ER and 777F.≈60→ BABritish AirwaysLaunch customer of the 777-200ER; 777-9s on order.≈57→ KEKorean AirPassenger and freighter fleets from Seoul Incheon.≈55→ FXFedEx ExpressThe largest 777F freighter fleet in the world.≈55→ CXCathay PacificLaunch customer of the 777-300.≈52→ TKTurkish Airlines≈41→
    TWEVA Air≈40
    CZChina Southern AirlinesPassenger and freighter.≈40→
    SASaudia≈38
    KLKLM≈31→ NHAll Nippon AirwaysA 777-9 launch customer; older -300s retiring by early 2027.≈30→ CAAir China≈28→
    INAir India≈28
    ACAir Canada25→
    RUAeroflotLong-haul widebody operator.≈24
    SQSingapore Airlines777-300ERs bridging to the 777-9 order.≈23→ MUChina Eastern Airlines20→
    THThai Airways≈20
    DEAeroLogicAll-freighter operation flying 777Fs for DHL.≈20
    TWChina AirlinesPassenger and freighter.≈19
    ETEthiopian AirlinesPassenger, -200LR and freighter fleets.≈19
    Other operatorsAround 40 more airlines and cargo carriers — from Swiss, Etihad, Asiana and Philippine Airlines to Cargolux, Lufthansa Cargo and the government VIP fleets.≈300

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
